macro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Rule on Four-by-Four Space and Reshut HaYachid

micro_segments.title: Revisiting Rav Chisda's statement about placement on a four-by-four space versus akira and hanacha
Tosafot's explanation: walls of reshut hayachid extend upward, eliminating the four-by-four requirement
The Gemara's connection to Rabbi's ruling on a tiny protrusion (ziz) in reshut hayachid
Abaye's concession: everyone agrees no four-by-four is needed in reshut hayachid for hanacha

macro_segments.title: The Tree with Branch Overhanging: Where Does the Object Reside?

micro_segments.title: Abaye reframes the dispute: the tree trunk is in reshut hayachid but the branch extends into reshut harabim
Rabbi holds the branch follows the trunk (shadinnofo batar ikaro); Rabbanan disagree
The core question: is an object on the branch considered part of reshut harabim or reshut hayachid?
Practical consequence: if branch is reshut harabim and not four-by-four, you're exempt; if trunk makes it reshut hayachid, you're liable
Parallel example: a fugitive on a branch of a tree whose trunk is in a city of refuge
Rashi's alternative explanation: the trunk gives significance to the branch as if it were four-by-four

macro_segments.title: The Beehive Case: Domains and Height Measurements

micro_segments.title: Abaye's beehive scenario: ten tefachim high but not six wide; determining if it constitutes a reshut hayachid
Geometry of reshut hayachid: four-by-four square requires ~5.6 diameter for circular objects
Rashi's view: you carry domains, not objects; a domain-sized beehive is exempt
Tosafot's alternative: exempt because you carried from reshut hayachid to reshut hayachid (the beehive itself)
Rava's objection: even if not six wide, parts extending above ten tefachim means not all in reshut harabim
Mouth facing down with lavud principle: 7+ tefachim triggers virtual connection to ground at three tefachim
Rav Ashi's distinction: vessel walls are not functional walls; lavud doesn't apply to non-structural walls

macro_segments.title: Pillars and Holes: Usage as the Key to Reshut HaRabim Status

micro_segments.title: Ulla's pillar case: nine tefachim high, used by the masses to arrange packages; is it reshut harabim?
Principle: below three tefachim is walked on; three to nine is unused space; nine and up is used for placing items
Abaye challenges Rava: what about a hole in the ground—does usage matter for holes?
Parallel to yesterday's debate: holes in walls and holes in ground; ease of use determines status
Rav Ada bar Matna's challenge to Rava: a basket ten tefachim high and four by four wide—is it reshut hayachid?
Resolution: if ten high and four by four, it is reshut hayachid; if smaller, just a basket (object), not a reshut
Rasha's principle: objects don't become karmelit or reshut unless they meet dimensional criteria for real domains

macro_segments.title: The Eruv in a Deep Pit: Accessibility and Reshut Status

micro_segments.title: Eruv techumin scenario: placing food in a pit to extend the 2,000-amot boundary
Problem: if pit is reshut hayachid, how can you access food from reshut harabim?
If pit less than ten tefachim, food in karmelit; if deeper, food in reshut hayachid
Proof from Beraita: if less than ten, eruv works, implying pit is reshut harabim even with difficulty
Rava's response: pit is karmelit, but food is edible bein hashmashot when drabanan don't apply per Rabbi
Rava reasserts: even if pit is karmelit, eruv works because drabanan suspended in twilight hours

macro_segments.title: Rava's Definition of Usage: Walking vs. Non-Walking Uses

micro_segments.title: Rava from Beraita on puddles: people walk through puddles even in rain; emphasizes 'passage' as primary reshut harabim function
Rava's distinction: hiluch (walking) al yedei hadachak counts as reshut harabim; tashmish (use) al yedei hadachak does not
Implication: things used for walking (even with difficulty) are reshut harabim; things used differently must be very convenient
Contrast with Abaye: Abaye accepts all uses; Rava distinguishes between walking (primary) and other uses (secondary)

macro_segments.title: Transporting Objects in Multiple Acts: Throwing and Setting Up a Bundle

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's zarza (bundle) case: throwing and standing up a tall bundle in reshut harabim
Until you pick it up (eker), you don't transgress; separate acts mean separate melachot
Practical application: carrying a tall object by moving parts separately, each outside the four-amot zone
Tosafot's question on baby carriages: continuous rolling (not separate acts) might be liable even without lifting
Contrast with dragging (kovel): even without lifting, continuous dragging out of place is akirah and liable

macro_segments.title: The Ledge (Iskofah): Definition of a Makom Patur (Exempted Zone)

micro_segments.title: Scenario: a ledge overhanging from reshut hayachid into reshut harabim, not four-by-four tefachim wide
Usage: transferring objects between householder (reshut hayachid) and poor person (reshut harabim) via the ledge
Gemara's elimination: not reshut harabim (not used by public), not reshut hayachid (not private structure), not karmelit (allowed)
Resolution: makom patur (exempted place)—a fourth domain, less than four-by-four, legally neutral space
Parameters: must be less than four by four tefachim to qualify; exempted from all reshut restrictions
Rav Dimi in name of Rabbi Yochanan: place without four-by-four is permitted for both domains to place objects, with restriction on passing through

topical_tags.term: Abaye vs. Rava on Holes and Usage
Hiluch al Yedei Hadachak (Walking with Difficulty)
Tashmish al Yedei Hadachak (Use with Difficulty)
Rashi on Carrying Domains vs. Objects
